methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 20 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
DroneShield operates in the Defense & Security sector as a public company, having raised over $100M to date. The comparable universe comprises 2 public peers (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 8 private competitors, including Anduril Industries, Saronic, Flock Safety, Shield AI, and Chaos Industries. This fragmented set reflects the nascent and heterogeneous nature of frontier defense technology markets.

Peer-median valuation methodology is not applicable to this analysis. The Defense & Security sector contains 20 same-sector peers overall, but filtering by stage alignment, adjacency, or valuation tier yields fewer than three datapoints in any narrow cohort—the minimum threshold for reliable triangulation. Applying an all-sector median would risk conflating companies with materially different business models, customer acquisition cycles, and regulatory profiles, introducing unacceptable noise into the valuation estimate.

Without a sufficient narrow peer set or executed triangulation, this analysis cannot support a specific valuation range or synthesized recommendation. The fragmentation of the comparable universe—particularly the absence of same-stage, similar-tier Defense & Security peers—is itself material information: it suggests DroneShield operates in a category with limited direct comparables. Any valuation work should prioritize building transaction-level or revenue multiple data from the private cohort (Anduril, Saronic, et al.) or deriving cost-of-capital and margin assumptions from first principles rather than relying on compressed peer multiples.
